skill.name: clean-code
skill.description: Clean code guidance for TypeScript and Node.js projects, with examples for naming, functions, errors, and organization.
skill.summary: Apply practical clean code principles to TypeScript and Node.js development.
skill.author: DoubleslashSE

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged external command, network, filesystem, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns. Review found these are false positives from Markdown code fences, TypeScript examples, regex literals, and generic API snippets. No executable scripts, prompt injection, credential access, or exfiltration behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The Ruby and shell backtick alerts are false positives. The reviewed locations are Markdown code fences or TypeScript template literals inside documentation, not shell execution by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are inside Markdown examples and are not executable skill logic. No command runner, shell invocation, or dynamic execution path was found.

low_findings.title: Example Network Calls Dismissed
low_findings.description: The fetch alerts and hardcoded URL are documentation examples for application code. They do not run when the skill is loaded and do not send user data to an external service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network indicators appear only in fenced TypeScript snippets showing ordinary API usage. No active network-capable script or instruction to exfiltrate data was found.

low_findings.title: Filesystem and Database Signals Dismissed
low_findings.description: The hidden file and SQLite-related alerts are false positives from regex characters and generic database examples. The skill does not read hidden files or open database files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited filesystem lines are email regex examples, and the database reference is an illustrative dependency-injection snippet. No filesystem API or database file access is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Dismissed
low_findings.description: No cryptographic implementation was found at the flagged locations. The alerts appear to be triggered by ordinary text, regex examples, or Markdown structure.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic code. The cited lines are documentation or TypeScript sample boundaries.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Dismissed
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts do not correspond to host inspection or environment probing. The cited lines are ordinary sample code about identifiers and collection updates.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines do not inspect the operating system, process list, host identity, or network configuration. No semantic reconnaissance behavior was found.

content.user_title: Apply Clean Code Practices to TypeScript
content.value_statement: TypeScript teams often struggle with duplicated logic, unclear names, and overbuilt abstractions. This skill gives practical clean code guidance for simpler, testable Node.js and TypeScript code.
actual_capabilities: Explains DRY, KISS, and YAGNI principles with TypeScript-focused examples.
Provides naming, function design, error handling, and formatting guidance.
Shows how to separate pure business logic from impure I/O code.
Recommends feature-based project organization for Node.js services.
Demonstrates typed errors, Result patterns, and dependency injection concepts.
limitations: It is a guidance skill, not an automated formatter or static analyzer.
Examples are TypeScript-oriented and may need adaptation for other languages.
It does not replace project-specific architecture decisions or team standards.
It does not execute tests, inspect repositories, or modify code by itself.

use_cases.title: Refactor TypeScript Services
use_cases.description: Identify duplicated logic, oversized functions, and unclear boundaries before changing production service code.

use_cases.title: Review Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Apply consistent clean code criteria when reviewing naming, error handling, and abstraction choices.

use_cases.title: Teach Maintainable Patterns
use_cases.description: Use concise examples to explain clean code concepts to developers learning TypeScript project structure.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic Clean Code Issues
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small TypeScript function.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this TypeScript function for clean code issues. Focus on naming, duplication, and simple control flow. Explain the top three improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a Service Method
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve one service method without changing behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this TypeScript service method using clean code principles. Preserve behavior, reduce nesting, improve names, and explain each change.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate Project Organization
prompt_templates.scenario: Assess module boundaries in a Node.js project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess this Node.js project structure for clean code concerns. Recommend feature grouping, dependency boundaries, and places to separate pure logic from I/O.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Refactoring Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for a risky codebase cleanup.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a staged refactoring plan for this TypeScript module. Prioritize tests, behavior preservation, dependency injection, typed errors, and measurable review checkpoints.

output_examples.input: A function mixes validation, persistence, notification, and logging in one long flow.
output: Separate validation, persistence, notification, and logging into named functions.
Keep the public behavior unchanged while adding focused tests around each step.
Use typed errors so callers can handle validation and permission failures clearly.

output_examples.input: A pull request introduces many configuration options before there is a real product need.
output: Mark the design as a likely YAGNI issue.
Keep only the options required by current behavior.
Document which future requirement would justify adding the removed options later.

output_examples.input: A feature module imports database and logger singletons directly from many files.
output: Recommend dependency injection at the composition root.
Move business rules into functions that can be tested without I/O dependencies.
Keep infrastructure wiring near the application entry point.

best_practices: Ask for behavior-preserving changes before requesting broad refactors.
Provide enough surrounding code to evaluate names, dependencies, and module boundaries.
Use the guidance with tests and project standards before changing production code.
anti_patterns: Do not request abstractions for hypothetical future requirements without evidence.
Do not apply every example mechanically across different languages or frameworks.
Do not treat clean code feedback as a substitute for security or performance review.

faq.question: What languages does this skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on TypeScript and Node.js, with patterns that may transfer to similar application code.

faq.question: Can it automatically refactor my code?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and review criteria that an AI coding tool can apply during a separate code-editing task.

faq.question: Does it include testing advice?
faq.answer: Yes. It encourages small functions, pure logic, typed errors, and dependency injection because those choices improve testability.

faq.question: Is it only for senior engineers?
faq.answer: No. The examples are direct enough for beginners and still useful for experienced reviewers.

faq.question: Does it replace a linter or formatter?
faq.answer: No. Linters enforce rules, while this skill explains design and maintainability choices.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found documentation examples only, with no executable scripts or confirmed malicious behavior.
